title: String Slicing Deep Dive
subtitle: Reverse, skip, and extract with the full power of slice notation
learning_objectives: Use the full string[start:stop:step] slice syntax
Explain why strings are immutable and what that means
Convert a string to a list of characters and join it back

sections.heading: The Full Slice Syntax: start, stop, step
sections.content:
You already know you can grab part of a string with word[start:stop]. Python slicing has a third parameter — step — making the full syntax word[start:stop:step].

The step controls how many characters to jump after each one you take. A step of 2 takes every other character. A step of -1 walks backwards through the string, which is how you reverse it.

Any part of the slice can be left blank. A blank start defaults to the beginning, a blank stop defaults to the end, and a blank step defaults to 1.
sections.code_example:
word = "abcdefgh"

# Every other character starting from index 0
print(word[::2])

# Characters at index 1, 3, 5
print(word[1::2])

# Reverse the whole string
print(word[::-1])

# Reverse a slice (chars 2-5, reversed)
print(word[2:6][::-1])
sections.note: word[::-1] is the idiomatic Python way to reverse a string. It is fast and concise.

sections.heading: Strings Are Immutable — Working Around It
sections.content:
Unlike lists, strings in Python cannot be changed in place. Trying to assign to an index raises a TypeError.

To "change" a string you must create a new one. A common pattern is to convert the string into a list of characters (using list()), modify the list, and then join the characters back with "".join().
sections.code_example:
word = "hello"

# This will cause a TypeError:
# word[0] = "H"

# Correct approach: list -> modify -> join
chars = list(word)
print(chars)

chars[0] = "H"
result = "".join(chars)
print(result)

# You can also build a new string with slicing
result2 = "H" + word[1:]
print(result2)
sections.note: list(word) splits a string into individual characters. "".join(chars) glues them back together with no separator.

exercises.title: Reverse and Sample
exercises.prompt: The variable text is set to "racecar". Print the string reversed using slice notation, then print every other character of the original string (starting from index 0).
exercises.starter_code:
text = "racecar"
# Print the string reversed using slice notation

# Print every other character of the string
hints: Use [::2] to get every other character.

tests.message: Your code should run without errors.
The reversed string should be printed (racecar is a palindrome, so it looks the same).
Every other character of 'racecar' starting at 0 is 'rcea'.

exercises.title: Fix a Character
exercises.prompt: The variable word is set to "python". Convert it to a list of characters, change the first character to "P" (uppercase), then join the list back into a string called fixed and print it.
exercises.starter_code:
word = "python"
# Convert to list
chars = list(word)
# Change the first character to "P"

# Create a variable called fixed by joining chars back into a string
# Print fixed
exercises.solution:
word = "python"
chars = list(word)
chars[0] = "P"
fixed = "".join(chars)
print(fixed)
hints: After list(word), use chars[0] = "P" to change the first item.
Use "".join(chars) to convert the list back to a string.

tests.message: Your code should run without errors.
The output should be 'Python' with a capital P.

quiz.question: What does "hello"[::2] evaluate to?
quiz.explanation: A step of 2 picks characters at indices 0, 2, 4 — which are 'h', 'l', 'o' — giving "hlo".

quiz.question: Why does word[0] = "H" raise a TypeError when word is a string?
options: Because strings are immutable and cannot be changed in place
Because you must use single quotes, not double quotes
quiz.explanation: Strings are immutable in Python. Once created, individual characters cannot be reassigned. You must build a new string instead.

completion_message: Great job! Slicing with a step is one of Python's most expressive tricks. Remember: [::-1] reverses any sequence and strings are immutable — work around that with list(), modify, then join().
